headline: Florida rewrites its property-tax math; New York goes after listing tech; Washington eyes institutional buyers.
paragraphs: Florida's headline measures this cycle are constitutional: H.1 and S.2 ('Save our Homes from Excessive Property Taxes') passed and put tighter assessment caps, a larger homestead exemption, and limits on local ad valorem taxation before voters — with H.203 going further, proposing to eliminate non-school property taxes on homesteads entirely. Underwriting Florida residential deals means watching these ballot outcomes.

New York's legislature is focused on how real estate is marketed and priced: bills would mandate public marketing of listings (A.10679/S.10274), ban algorithmic and dynamic pricing in sales and rentals (A.11490), and make misleading AI-enhanced listing images a license-revocation offense (A.11403/S.9584). A.5499 would ban dual agency and mandatory broker fees outright.

In Washington, S.3937 would use antitrust law to bar large institutional entities from buying single-family homes, while the USDA Loan Modernization Act (H.R. 6779/S.3936) eases entity-based eligibility for farm real estate loans. Pennsylvania's most consequential move is a full rewrite of its real estate licensing law (SB 907/HB 2645), adding home-inspector licensing.
